docs.text: Core CS Concepts This page assumes you know nothing about programming or computer science (CS) and lays a foundation you can build your knowledge upon. What is a computer? A computer is made up of: A mother board (Main circuit board that other circuit boards/drives are plugged into) A CPU (Collection of processors that perform certain functions, given certain code) RAM (Short-term memory) Storage Device (Long-term memory) Graphics card (Like a CPU, but used for graphical rendering onto a monitor) A computer is designed to take a set of instructions (code) and perform tasks outlined in those intructions. What is a server? A server is a type of computer, made of the same components listed above. In most households, we have Personal Computers. These are designed with sufficient resources (storage, memory, processing power) to perform tasks that everyday individuals need to do, as well as be cost-efficient so every household can afford one. Servers are much more powerful (and expensive) computers, that are designed for more complex tasks, like hosting a social media application. What is "The Cloud"? "The Cloud" is a term given to a collections of one or more servers that are typically accessible to people. There are big buildings all over the world that each contain many servers. When you "upload a picture to the cloud", you are actually sending your picture to one of these servers to store. How does a computer read code? A computer, more specifically a processor (CPU), is designed to read something called "Machine Code". Machine code is impossible for humans to write, so we've developed "higher-level" languages, like JavaScript, Python, C++, etc. These languages are much easier to write, but they need to be translated into Machine Code before a computer can understand it. This is done with an "Interpreter" and/or a "Compiler" What are Interpreters and/or Compilers? Compilers are little programs that take code you write and translate it into machine code. Machine code is "archiceture specific". This means if you compile your code on one computer, it will only be able to run on similar computers. For example: Code that is compiled on a Mac, can not run on a PC. The flow would be something like this: You write some code to a file, you run a compiler and give it that files, it compiles it into Machine Code and saves it to another file called an "Executable". You can then run your executable just like any other program. Interpreters are similar, in that they translate the code you write into Machine Code, however they work a little differently. They translate the code and execute it as your program runs. The flow would be something like this: You write some code to a file, you run the interpreter and give it your file, it translates the file and runs the Machine Code through the processor in realtime. Examples of compiled languages are C, C++, Go, Rust. Examples of interpreted languages are Python, JavaScript, Ruby. Note : Interpretted languages are generally "slower" to run than compiled, because the process of translation is longer, however interpretted languages are typically easier to learn and use and therefore quicker to use in development.
docs.title: Core CS Concepts

docs.text: FAQ What language should I learn first? I will state that it is MY OPINION that a person should start learning programming with the language "Typescript" . It is high level enough that you won't get bogged down by minute details, it uses a compiler and an interperator, it can be used server-side and client-side, it's widely supported by common engineering tools, etc. Is [insert any language] dead? No. It's still used. However you are probably asking this because you want to sit down and memorize a language. Instead you should be focusing on learning the topic that interests you, and you should learn/utilize the language that fits that as you develop. Programming does not revolve around a language. Languages are just tools to be used when needed. I'm learning but have no project ideas, what should I make? A blog web app that allows people to create their own accounts and manage blog posts via user interface (browser) and API. You will learn about front-end, back-end, database management, http requests, security authentication, server hosting, etc.
docs.title: FAQ

docs.text: What language should I learn first? I will state that it is MY OPINION that a person should start learning programming with the language "Typescript" . It is high level enough that you won't get bogged down by minute details, it uses a compiler and an interperator, it can be used server-side and client-side, it's widely supported by common engineering tools, etc. Is [insert any language] dead? No. It's still used. However you are probably asking this because you want to sit down and memorize a language. Instead you should be focusing on learning the topic that interests you, and you should learn/utilize the language that fits that as you develop. Programming does not revolve around a language. Languages are just tools to be used when needed. I'm learning but have no project ideas, what should I make? A blog web app that allows people to create their own accounts and manage blog posts via user interface (browser) and API. You will learn about front-end, back-end, database management, http requests, security authentication, server hosting, etc.
docs.title: FAQ

docs.text: The Code Aid Mission tl;dr Code Aid provides short, sweet lessons with no big school words. Everything should be "tl;dr" ts;wm I've created Code Aid because I saw a dire need in the Software Engineering community for a knowledge supplement. Usually, from the first thought, "I want to be a software engineer" to around the fifth year into one's career, there are tons of questions. I've noticed three outcomes in this scenario: 1) the person is too shy to ask questions and is unsuccessful, 2) the person asks questions and gets convoluted, pedantic answers that confuse them even further, 3) the person spends more time than they should trying to answer their question. -- Enter Code Aid. -- I strive to answer your questions before they become questions. I focus on efficient, applied answers to get you moving, advancing, and building projects sooner. The beginning of one's career is not the time to be a Pedant. That can come later. Let's just get you educated .
docs.title: Mission

docs.text: Code Aid provides short, sweet lessons with no big school words. Everything should be "tl;dr"

docs.text: Start Just a few notes before you dive into what is offered here. This is not a "learn xyz language" resource. Languages are tools and should be used/studied as such. Also, don't waste too much time memorizing a language, chances are you'll only be using it a little while. "Best" is a relative term. My "best" language is not yours. My "best" cloud provider is not yours. Your "best" {thing} is what you put the most effort into. Code Aid is meant to be both a reference source as well as a guide. You can follow the categories chronologically (in order) and/or bookmark a page to reference a topic later. This is not a "one stop shop" of being an engineer (those don't exist). You will read docs, you will Google, you will ask people you know, etc. Code Aid is intended only as a solid "jump start" into software development that won't leave your understanding of topics lacking. The ultimate goal is keeping lessons short and sweet with no "big words".
docs.title: Start Here

docs.text: The Terminal The act of interacting with a computer is called "interfacing" and as such you need an "interface" to do so. There are two types of interfaces: 1. Graphical User Interface (GUI) 2. The Terminal Most people are familiar with interacting with their computer using the GUI. For example, when you use your mouse to click on an icon, drag a file to folder, view a picture, etc. you (the " U ser") are " I nterfacing" with the " G raphical" portion of your computer. The Terminal, also known as a "shell", is another way of interacting with your computer. Instead of using your mouse to click around, you type "commands" into the terminal and
docs.title: The Terminal
